pathophysiology_summary: Human papillomavirus (typically HPV types 1, 2, 4) infects plantar keratinocytes after inoculation through macerated skin — classically acquired barefoot on communal wet surfaces (pool/shower floors). Under the constant vertical load of weight-bearing the lesion grows endophytically (inward) rather than exophytically, producing a firm hyperkeratotic papule that disrupts the normal dermatoglyphic skin lines. Dilated, thrombosed dermal capillaries appear as the classic pinpoint black dots once the overlying callus is pared. Because the lesion is compressible tissue rather than a keratin plug, pain is provoked more by lateral (pinch/squeeze) compression than by direct vertical pressure — the reverse of a plantar corn. It is benign and frequently self-limiting (many resolve spontaneously over months to two years), but is often painful and slow to clear.

differential.evidence_basis: Firm hyperkeratotic plantar papule, slowly enlarging, painful on weight-bearing, with communal wet-floor exposure.
The patient's own label ('corn'); a common mimic. Distinguished by intact skin lines and pain on direct rather than lateral pressure.
Plantar pain and a firm nodule can follow an embedded foreign body; no penetrating-trauma history here.
Pain on lateral (pinch) compression, communal-shower exposure, and poor response to a corn plaster all favour a wart over a corn.
Less likely once lateral-compression pain is elicited, but cannot be fully excluded without paring.

differential.diagnosis: Deep soft-tissue abscess / infected foreign body
differential.evidence_basis: No fever, no spreading erythema, no fluctuance or discharge reported — argues strongly against a pyogenic collection.
Must be actively excluded before recommending a keratolytic acid — patient denies diabetes and reports normal foot sensation, lowering probability.
Classic history plus (on in-person confirmation) disrupted skin lines and thrombosed capillaries on paring. Benign, self-limiting, telehealth-manageable with self-care and routine podiatry.
Rare, but any atypical, pigmented, bleeding or non-resolving plantar lesion warrants dermatological/podiatric review rather than indefinite self-treatment.

red_flags.description: Signs of secondary infection / ascending lymphangitis — spreading redness, red streaks up the foot, warmth, swelling, purulent discharge, or fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would convert a benign lesion into a time-sensitive soft-tissue infection needing same-day antibiotics or ED assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated cellulitis/lymphangitis can progress to systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: Diabetes, peripheral neuropathy, or peripheral vascular disease affecting the foot
red_flags.clinical_significance: A neuropathic/ischaemic foot reclassifies the lesion as a high-risk ulcer and CONTRAINDICATES unsupervised salicylic-acid keratolytics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Salicylic acid on an insensate/ischaemic foot can cause chemical ulceration, non-healing wounds and, ultimately, amputation risk.

red_flags.description: Atypical, pigmented, bleeding, or persistently non-healing plantar lesion (possible amelanotic melanoma)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The important-not-to-miss malignancy behind a 'benign plantar lump'; classic viral features here make it unlikely but do not exclude it over time.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed diagnosis of plantar melanoma worsens prognosis substantially.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting the patient's 'corn' label. A corn preserves skin lines and hurts on direct vertical pressure; a wart disrupts skin lines, shows thrombosed capillaries on paring, and hurts on lateral (pinch) compression.
Being pulled by the source note's 'urgent after-hours home visit' framing into over-triaging a benign, self-limiting lesion to same-day/urgent care or ED.
Recommending salicylic acid without first excluding diabetes, neuropathy or peripheral vascular disease — dangerous on an insensate or ischaemic foot.
Labelling every plantar lump a wart and never safety-netting the atypical/non-resolving lesion that needs review for amelanotic melanoma.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Paring of the callus to reveal thrombosed capillaries (the confirmatory manoeuvre)
Close inspection of disruption of the dermatoglyphic skin lines
Direct palpation, and true depth or fixation of the lesion
Formal neurovascular examination of the foot (sensation, pulses, capillary refill)
Dermoscopy or biopsy if malignancy is suspected
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: No emergency action required. Reassure, start self-care (keratolytic + filing + offloading padding + footwear hygiene), refer routinely to podiatry for paring and cryotherapy, and safety-net for secondary infection and for an atypical/non-healing lesion.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the benign diagnosis; the genuine risks are OVER-action (over-triaging to urgent/ED) and failing to screen for a diabetic/neuropathic foot before recommending an acid, or failing to safety-net melanoma.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Australian general-practice management of cutaneous and plantar warts (self-care keratolytics, cryotherapy, watchful waiting)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Most plantar warts are benign and self-limiting; first-line treatment is topical salicylic acid with regular paring/filing, with cryotherapy as an adjunct; avoid keratolytics on the diabetic or ischaemic foot.
